stem: A [[m]]-scaled 3-4-5 ladder of length \( L \) (with \( L^{2}=[[L2]] \)) leans against a wall, with its base \( x=[[x0]] \) ft from the wall and its top \( y=[[y0]] \) ft up the wall (so \( x^{2}+y^{2}=L^{2} \)). The base slides away from the wall at \( \dfrac{dx}{dt}=[[dxdt]] \) ft/s. At this instant, what is \( \dfrac{dy}{dt} \) (in ft/s)?
solution: Differentiating \( x^{2}+y^{2}=L^{2} \) gives \( 2x\dfrac{dx}{dt}+2y\dfrac{dy}{dt}=0 \), so \( \dfrac{dy}{dt}=-\dfrac{x}{y}\dfrac{dx}{dt} \). At this instant, \( \dfrac{dy}{dt} = -\dfrac{[[x0]]}{[[y0]]}\cdot [[dxdt]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign that shows the top is sliding DOWN as the base slides away.
This inverts the ratio \( x/y \), using \( y/x \) instead.
This forgets to scale by the ratio \( x/y \) and just reports \( -dx/dt \).
This reports the ratio \( x/y \) itself, forgetting to multiply by \( dx/dt \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A circular oil slick has radius \( r \), area \( A=\pi r^{2} \), and its radius is growing at \( \dfrac{dr}{dt}=[[drdt]] \) m/s. At the instant \( r=[[r0]] \) m, what is \( \dfrac{dA}{dt} \) (in \( \text{m}^{2}/\text{s} \))?
solution: Differentiating \( A=\pi r^{2} \) gives \( \dfrac{dA}{dt}=2\pi r\dfrac{dr}{dt} \). At \( r=[[r0]] \), \( \dfrac{dA}{dt} = 2\pi([[r0]])([[drdt]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 that comes from differentiating \( r^{2} \).
This forgets to multiply by \( dr/dt \) entirely.
This uses \( r^{2} \) instead of \( r \) after differentiating, undoing the power rule.
This forgets the factor of \( \pi \) entirely.
This is off by \( \pi \) from the correct value.
This is off by \( \pi \) from the correct value.

stem: Water drains into a conical tank (vertex down) whose radius is always \( \dfrac{1}{[[k]]} \) of its water height \( h \), so the volume is \( V=\dfrac{1}{3}\pi\left(\dfrac{h}{[[k]]}\right)^{2}h=\dfrac{\pi h^{3}}{3\cdot[[k]]^{2}} \). If \( \dfrac{dV}{dt}=[[dVdt]] \) \( \text{m}^{3}/\text{min} \), what is \( \dfrac{dh}{dt} \) at the instant \( h=[[h0]] \) m?
solution: Differentiating \( V=\dfrac{\pi h^{3}}{3\cdot[[k]]^{2}} \) gives \( \dfrac{dV}{dt}=\dfrac{\pi h^{2}}{[[k]]^{2}}\dfrac{dh}{dt} \). Solving for \( \dfrac{dh}{dt} \) at \( h=[[h0]] \) gives \( \dfrac{dh}{dt}=\dfrac{[[dVdt]]\,[[k]]^{2}}{\pi([[h0]])^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square \( k \) in the numerator.
This uses \( k \) instead of \( k^{2} \) and \( h_{0} \) instead of \( h_{0}^{2} \).
This uses \( h_{0} \) instead of \( h_{0}^{2} \) in the denominator.
This multiplies by \( \pi \) instead of dividing by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Two cars leave an intersection, one heading east and one heading north. At a certain instant the eastbound car is \( x=[[x0]] \) mi from the intersection moving at \( \dfrac{dx}{dt}=[[dxdt]] \) mi/h, and the northbound car is \( y=[[y0]] \) mi from the intersection moving at \( \dfrac{dy}{dt}=[[dydt]] \) mi/h. If \( z \) is the distance between the cars (so \( z^{2}=x^{2}+y^{2}\), \( z=[[z0]] \) at this instant), what is \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} \) (in mi/h)?
solution: Differentiating \( z^{2}=x^{2}+y^{2} \) gives \( 2z\dfrac{dz}{dt} = 2x\dfrac{dx}{dt}+2y\dfrac{dy}{dt} \), so \( \dfrac{dz}{dt}=\dfrac{x\,dx/dt+y\,dy/dt}{z} \). At this instant, \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} = \dfrac{[[x0]]\cdot[[dxdt]]+[[y0]]\cdot[[dydt]]}{[[z0]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts instead of adds the two contributions in the numerator.
This simply adds the two given rates, ignoring the \( x,y,z \) weighting entirely.
This forgets to divide by \( z \), dividing by \( z^{2} \) instead.
This uses \( x \) and \( y \) directly instead of their rates \( dx/dt \), \( dy/dt \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: An observer stands \( [[x0]] \) m from the base of a launch pad, watching a rocket rise straight up. When the rocket is \( y=[[y0]] \) m high (so the line of sight has length \( z=[[z0]] \) m), the rocket's height is increasing at \( \dfrac{dy}{dt}=[[dydt]] \) m/s. Since \( \tan(\theta) = \dfrac{y}{[[x0]]} \) where \( \theta \) is the angle of elevation, what is \( \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} \) at this instant (in rad/s)?
solution: Differentiating \( \tan(\theta)=\dfrac{y}{[[x0]]} \) gives \( \sec^{2}(\theta)\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{[[x0]]}\dfrac{dy}{dt} \), so \( \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{[[x0]]}\dfrac{dy}{dt}\cos^{2}(\theta) \). Since \( \cos(\theta)=[[x0]]/[[z0]] \), this is \( \dfrac{[[dydt]]}{[[x0]]}\left(\dfrac{[[x0]]}{[[z0]]}\right)^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to multiply by \( \cos^{2}(\theta) \) entirely, leaving the un-corrected value \( 1/\sec^{2}(\theta) \) applied.
This inverts \( \cos^{2}(\theta) \), using \( \sec^{2}(\theta) \) instead.
This divides by \( y \) instead of the fixed distance \( x \).
This forgets the leading factor of \( 1/x \) from differentiating \( \tan(\theta)=y/x \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
